This article contains **[phonetic transcriptions](/wiki/Phonetic_transcription "Phonetic transcription") in the [International Phonetic Alphabet](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet") (IPA)**. For an introductory guide on IPA symbols, see [Help:IPA](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA"). For the distinction between [ ], / / and ⟨ ⟩, see [IPA § Brackets and transcription delimiters](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet#Brackets_and_transcription_delimiters "International Phonetic Alphabet").

The **Linguistic Society of Hong Kong Cantonese Romanization Scheme**,[[a]](#cite_note-1) also known as **Jyutping**, is a [romanisation](/wiki/Romanization "Romanization") system for [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") developed in 1993 by the [Linguistic Society of Hong Kong](/wiki/Linguistic_Society_of_Hong_Kong "Linguistic Society of Hong Kong") (LSHK).

The name *Jyutping* (itself the Jyutping romanisation of its Chinese name, 粵拼) is a [contraction](/wiki/Contraction_(grammar) "Contraction (grammar)") of the official name, and it consists of the first [Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_characters "Chinese characters") of the terms *jyut6 jyu5* ([Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 粵語; lit. '[Cantonese language](/wiki/Yue_Chinese "Yue Chinese")') and *ping3 jam1* ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 拼音; lit. 'phonetic alphabet'; pronounced *pīnyīn* in [Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese")).

Despite being intended as a system to indicate pronunciation, it has also been employed in [writing Cantonese as an alphabetic language](/wiki/Romanization_of_Cantonese "Romanization of Cantonese") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%B2%B5%E6%96%87%E6%8B%89%E4%B8%81%E5%8C%96 "zh:粵文拉丁化")]—in effect, elevating Jyutping from its assistive status to a written language.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jyutping&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The Jyutping system[[1]](#cite_note-2) departs from all previous Cantonese romanisation systems (approximately 12, including [Robert Morrison's](/wiki/Robert_Morrison_(missionary) "Robert Morrison (missionary)") pioneering work of 1828, and the widely used [Standard Romanization](/wiki/Standard_Romanization_(Cantonese) "Standard Romanization (Cantonese)"), [Yale](/wiki/Yale_romanization_of_Cantonese "Yale romanization of Cantonese") and [Sidney Lau](/wiki/Sidney_Lau_romanisation "Sidney Lau romanisation") systems) by introducing z and c initials and the use of eo and oe in finals, as well as replacing the initial y, used in all previous systems, with j.[[2]](#cite_note-3)

In 2018, it was updated to include the -a and -oet finals, to reflect syllables recognized as part of Cantonese phonology in 1997 by the Jyutping Work Group of the Linguistic Society of Hong Kong.[[3]](#cite_note-lshkjyutping-4)

## Tones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jyutping&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Tones")]

Main article: [Cantonese phonology § Tones](/wiki/Cantonese_phonology#Tones "Cantonese phonology")

There are nine [tones](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)") in six distinct [tone contours](/wiki/Tone_contour "Tone contour") in Cantonese. However, as three of the nine are [entering tones](/wiki/Checked_tone "Checked tone") (入聲; *jap6 sing1*), which only appear in syllables ending with *p*, *t*, and *k*, they do not have separate tone numbers in Jyutping (though they do in the [ILE romanization of Cantonese](/wiki/ILE_romanization_of_Cantonese "ILE romanization of Cantonese"); these are shown in parentheses in the table below). A mnemonic which some use to remember this is 風水到時我哋必發達; *fung1 seoi2 dou3 si4 ngo5 dei6 bit1 faat3 daat6* or "[Feng Shui](/wiki/Feng_shui "Feng shui") [dictates that] we will be lucky."

| [Tone name](/wiki/Tone_name "Tone name") | jam1 ping4 (陰平) | | jam1 soeng5 (陰上) | | jam1 heoi3 (陰去) | | joeng4 ping4 (陽平) | | joeng4 soeng5 (陽上) | | joeng4 heoi3 (陽去) | | gou1 jam1 jap6 (高陰入) | | dai1 jam1 jap6 (低陰入) | | joeng4 jap6 (陽入) | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| (In English) | high level or high falling | | mid rising | | mid level | | low falling | | low rising | | low level | | entering high level | | entering mid level | | entering low level | |
| Tone number | 1 | | 2 | | 3 | | 4 | | 5 | | 6 | | 1 (7) | | 3 (8) | | 6 (9) | |
| [Contour](/wiki/Tone_contour "Tone contour")[[4]](#cite_note-5) | ˥ 55 | ˥˧ 53 | ˧˥ 35 | | ˧ 33 | | ˨˩ 21 | ˩ 11 | ˩˧ 13 | | ˨ 22 | | ˥ 5 | | ˧ 3 | | ˨ 2 | |
| Character example | 分 | 詩 | 粉 | 史 | 訓 | 試 | 焚 | 時 | 奮 | 市 | 份 | 是 | 忽 | 識 | 發 | 錫 | 佛 | 食 |
| fan1 | si1 | fan2 | si2 | fan3 | si3 | fan4 | si4 | fan5 | si5 | fan6 | si6 | fat1 | sik1 | faat3 | sek3 | fat6 | sik6 |

## Comparison with Yale romanisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jyutping&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Comparison with Yale romanisation")]

Jyutping and the [Yale romanisation of Cantonese](/wiki/Yale_romanization_of_Cantonese "Yale romanization of Cantonese") represent Cantonese pronunciations with the same letters in:

* The [initials](/wiki/Initial_(linguistics) "Initial (linguistics)"): *b*, *p*, *m*, *f*, *d*, *t*, *n*, *l*, *g*, *k*, *ng*, *h*, *s*, *gw*, *kw*, *w*.
* The [vowel](/wiki/Vowel "Vowel"): *aa* (except when used alone), *a*, *e*, *i*, *o*, *u*, *yu*.
* The [nasal stop](/wiki/Nasal_stop "Nasal stop"): *m*, *ng*.
* The [coda](/wiki/Syllable_coda "Syllable coda"): *i*, *u*, *m*, *n*, *ng*, *p*, *t*, *k*.

But they differ in the following:

* The [vowels](/wiki/Vowel "Vowel") *eo* and *oe* represent /ɵ/ and /œː/ respectively in Jyutping, whereas the *eu* represents both vowels in Yale.
* The [initial](/wiki/Initial_(linguistics) "Initial (linguistics)") *j* represents /j/ in Jyutping whereas *y* is used instead in Yale.
* The initial *z* represents /ts/ in Jyutping whereas *j* is used instead in Yale.
* The initial *c* represents /tsʰ/ in Jyutping whereas *ch* is used instead in Yale.
* In Jyutping, if no [consonant](/wiki/Consonant "Consonant") precedes the vowel *yu*, then the initial *j* is appended before the vowel. In Yale, the corresponding initial *y* is never appended before *yu* under any circumstances.
* Jyutping defines five [finals](/wiki/Final_(linguistics) "Final (linguistics)") not in Yale: *a* /ɐ/*, eu* /ɛːu/, *em* /ɛːm/, *ep* /ɛːp/, oet /œːt/. These finals are used in colloquial Cantonese words, such as *deu6* (掉), *lem2* (舐), and *gep6* (夾).
* To represent [tones](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)"), only tone numbers are used in Jyutping whereas Yale traditionally uses tone marks together with the letter *h* (though tone numbers can be used in Yale as well).

## Comparison with ILE romanisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jyutping&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Comparison with ILE romanisation")]

Jyutping and [ILE romanisation](/wiki/ILE_romanization_of_Cantonese "ILE romanization of Cantonese") represent Cantonese pronunciations with the same letters in:

* The [initials](/wiki/Initial_(linguistics) "Initial (linguistics)"): *b*, *p*, *m*, *f*, *d*, *t*, *n*, *l*, *g*, *k*, *ng*, *h*, *s*, *gw*, *kw*, *j*, *w*.
* The [vowel](/wiki/Vowel "Vowel"): *aa*, *a*, *e*, *i*, *o*, *u*.
* The [nasal stop](/wiki/Nasal_stop "Nasal stop"): *m*, *ng*.
* The [coda](/wiki/Syllable_coda "Syllable coda"): *i* (except for its use in the [coda](/wiki/Syllable_coda "Syllable coda") /y/ in Jyutping; see below), *u*, *m*, *n*, *ng*, *p*, *t*, *k*.

But they have some differences:

* The [vowel](/wiki/Vowel "Vowel") *oe* represents both /ɵ/ and /œː/ in ILE whereas *eo* and *oe* represent /ɵ/ and /œː/ respectively in Jyutping.
* The vowel *y* represents /y/ in ILE whereas both *yu* (used in the [nucleus](/wiki/Syllable_nucleus "Syllable nucleus")) and *i* (used in the [coda](/wiki/Syllable_coda "Syllable coda") of the [final](/wiki/Syllable_rime#Syllable_final "Syllable rime") -*eoi*) are used in Jyutping.
* The initial *dz* represents /ts/ in ILE whereas *z* is used instead in Jyutping.
* The initial *ts* represents /tsʰ/ in ILE whereas *c* is used instead in Jyutping.
* To represent [tones](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)"), the numbers 1 to 9 are usually used in ILE, although the use of 1, 3, 6 to replace 7, 8, 9 for the [checked tones](/wiki/Checked_tone "Checked tone") is acceptable. However, only the numbers 1 to 6 are used in Jyutping.

## Examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jyutping&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Examples")]

| [Traditional](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_character "Traditional Chinese character") | [Simplified](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_character "Simplified Chinese character") | Romanization |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 廣州話 | 广州话 | gwong2 zau1 waa2 |
| 粵語 | 粤语 | jyut6 jyu5 |
| 你好 | 你好 | nei5 hou2 |

Sample transcription of one of the [*300 Tang Poems*](/wiki/Three_Hundred_Tang_Poems "Three Hundred Tang Poems"):

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [春曉](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%98%A5%E6%9B%89 "wikt:春曉") [孟浩然](/wiki/Meng_Haoran "Meng Haoran") | ceon1 hiu2 maang6 hou6 jin4 | Meng Haoran |
| 春眠不覺曉， | ceon1 min4 bat1 gok3 hiu2, | *Sleeping past sunrise in springtime.* |
| 處處聞啼鳥。 | cyu3 cyu3 man4 tai4 niu5. | *Everywhere one hears birdsong.* |
| 夜來風雨聲， | je6 loi4 fung1 jyu5 sing1, | *Night brings the sound of wind and rain,* |
| 花落知多少？ | faa1 lok6 zi1 do1 siu2? | *I wonder how many flowers fell?* |

## Jyutping input method

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jyutping&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Jyutping input method")]

A Jyutping [input method](/wiki/Chinese_input_methods_for_computers "Chinese input methods for computers") allows a user to type Chinese characters by entering their Jyutping romanization (with or without tone, depending on the system) and then presenting the user with a list of possible characters with that pronunciation.

As of [macOS Ventura](/wiki/MacOS_Ventura "MacOS Ventura") and [iOS 16](/wiki/IOS_16 "IOS 16"), Jyutping input in [traditional Chinese characters](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters") is a built-in functionality on Mac under the name "Phonetic – Cantonese" and on iPhone and iPad under the name "Cantonese".

### List of Jyutping keyboard input utilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jyutping&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: List of Jyutping keyboard input utilities")]

* [TypeDuck](https://www.typeduck.hk/) (TypeDuck 打得粵拼輸入法)
* [Online Jyutping Input Method](https://www.jyutping.com) (網上粵拼輸入法)
* [MDBG Type Chinese](https://www.mdbg.net/chinese/webime?ime=jyutping_trad)
* [Cantonese Phonetic IME](https://www.cpime.hk/p/cantonese-pinyin-input-software.html?lang=en) (廣東話拼音輸入法, also called 'Cantonese Phonetic IME (CPIME) Jyutping' in Windows 10)[[5]](#cite_note-6)
* [RIME](/w/index.php?title=RIME_(IME)&action=edit&redlink=1 "RIME (IME) (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%B8%AD%E5%B7%9E%E9%9F%BB%E8%BC%B8%E5%85%A5%E6%B3%95%E5%BC%95%E6%93%8E "zh:中州韻輸入法引擎")] (小狼毫輸入法引擎)
* [Jyutping Cantonese Keyboard App](https://github.com/yuetyam/jyutping) for iOS and macOS
* [Gboard](/wiki/Gboard "Gboard") by Google for Android and iOS
